metadata.from: Bob (Robert Hanifen) — signed 'Love Bob' at the end
metadata.date: Oct. 476 [likely a postmark annotation, possibly Oct. 4 '76 or similar partial date]
metadata.location_written_from: Military base/infirmary, likely near Cheyenne, Wyoming area (mentions U.P. railroad, Cheyenne)
metadata.location_sent_to: 1940 Overton Blvd [?], Des Moines, Iowa (envelope address)
metadata.postmark: Oct/96 476 / 9:57 AM in at 11e / 1:30 PM — annotations on envelope back; likely postmark notes
metadata.envelope_notes: Envelope back shows handwritten annotations: 'Oct/96 476', '9:57 AM in at 11e', '1:30 PM', 'S.M. 1992' (possibly Des Moines 1992 or a tracking note), '1940 Overton Blvd' as address. These may be later annotations or postal routing notes. Address reads approximately '1940 Overton Blvd'.
topics: travel itinerary to Des Moines and Ames Iowa
request to be picked up at Ames Iowa
people_mentioned: a Kid (unnamed soldier who went to Denver)
metadata.mood_tone: Concerned and a bit anxious — Bob has been sick, is worried about finals, but is excited about an upcoming furlough and eager to get home; practical and direct in tone
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is generally readable but some words are blurred or faded due to paper aging and ink bleed-through from the other side. Several words required guessing. The envelope annotations are particularly difficult to parse. The illness name on page 1 reads like 'Gastural' or 'Gastro-[enteritis]' — likely gastroenteritis.
metadata.historical_context: Letter appears to be from WWII era military service. Bob is on a military base, references G.I. exams, a Captain, furlough rate tickets, and a large epidemic at the base. The Union Pacific 'Challenger' was a famous train of the era. The mention of furlough travel and military testing suggests wartime military training context.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Bob is in the infirmary with gastroenteritis, has a furlough rate ticket on the Union Pacific Challenger (a famous train running through Cheyenne), and is traveling to Ames, Iowa at 1:10 AM Sunday. He mentions G.I. and department finals and the risk of flunking out ('if I don't the government pays your way home'). References to a Captain, furlough tickets, and academic finals are consistent with the ASTP program at University of Wyoming, Laramie (near Cheyenne). The annotation 'Oct. 476' is likely a later date notation in an unknown hand, not the letter's date. Given that the University of Wyoming ASTP letters span summer–fall 1944, and finals would occur at end of term, and Letter 18a mentions a leave September 30–October 10, this letter likely predates that leave — placing it in late September 1944. Alternatively, the notation may reference October and the letter could be from early October 1944. Estimated September–October 1944.

pages.transcription:
[Back of envelope, handwritten annotations]
Oct/96. 476
9:57 AM in at 11e
1:30 PM
S.M. 1992

[Address on lower portion:]
1940 Overton
Blvd [?]

Dear Dad

Sorry I haven't wrote but have been in the infirmary since Tues. about 3:00 I got sick and vomited all night darn near dead then had the G.I. till Sat. morn. I still feel pretty low. About 60 of us had it. They called [it] Gastural [Gastroenteritis?] and it goes [?] blue. Maybe you got it they had a big epidemic of it around here. I got real good news for you. A Kid went to Denver and he got me a furlough rate ticket on the U.P. would have

got in Des Moines 1:30 Sun. afternoon, but the Capt. informed us we get at 6:00 Sat morn. instead of 10:00 So I went down to the station and [crossed out word] found out it is good on the Challenger which [leaves] at 8:00 Sat morn. Only had to pay $1.50 extra to go to Cheyenne on the train. Altogether it cost me about $18.30. Get in Omaha at 9:30 and will be in Ames Sun. morn. at 1:10. I was wondering if you could meet me or maybe you could leave one of the cars there.

Have been taking our G.I. final this week and our Department finals and they are really tough baby to handle. I could have flunked out [crossed out word] but I think I will make it. if I don't the government pays your way home. Well I got a lot of studying to do so I will sign off for now.

Love Bob

P.S. Remember 1:10 Sun. morn. at Ames, Iowa

full_transcription:
Dear Dad

Sorry I haven't wrote but have been in the infirmary since Tues. about 3:00 I got sick and vomited all night darn near dead then had the G.I. till Sat. morn. I still feel pretty low. About 60 of us had it. They called [it] Gastural [Gastroenteritis?] and it goes [?] blue. Maybe you got it they had a big epidemic of it around here. I got real good news for you. A Kid went to Denver and he got me a furlough rate ticket on the U.P. would have got in Des Moines 1:30 Sun. afternoon, but the Capt. informed us we get at 6:00 Sat morn. instead of 10:00 So I went down to the station and [crossed out word] found out it is good on the Challenger which [leaves] at 8:00 Sat morn. Only had to pay $1.50 extra to go to Cheyenne on the train. Altogether it cost me about $18.30. Get in Omaha at 9:30 and will be in Ames Sun. morn. at 1:10. I was wondering if you could meet me or maybe you could leave one of the cars there.

Have been taking our G.I. final this week and our Department finals and they are really tough baby to handle. I could have flunked out [crossed out word] but I think I will make it. if I don't the government pays your way home. Well I got a lot of studying to do so I will sign off for now.

Love Bob

P.S. Remember 1:10 Sun. morn. at Ames, Iowa
summary: Bob writes to his father from a military base infirmary, where he has been sick since Tuesday with what appears to be a gastrointestinal illness that affected about 60 soldiers in what the letter calls a 'big epidemic.' Despite feeling low, he shares good news: a fellow soldier secured him a furlough rate ticket on the Union Pacific Challenger train, and he provides a detailed travel itinerary arriving in Ames, Iowa on Sunday morning at 1:10 AM, asking his father to meet him or leave a car. He also mentions taking difficult G.I. and department final exams, noting he thinks he will pass but that if he fails, the government pays his way home.
